id_brackets.note: Idaho Rules of Family Law Procedure, Rule 120(g)(1): the Basic Child Support obligation is computed as a marginal bracket schedule over ANNUAL combined Guidelines (gross) Income -- each bracket's percentage applies only to the portion of income within that bracket, like a tax bracket, summed across all filled brackets plus a prorated partial final bracket. Verified exactly against the rule's own worked example (2 children, $25,000 + $10,000 = $35,000 combined annual income -> $709/month total, $506/month paid by the higher earner). The 4-children table's published final bracket width ($20,000) is very likely a publishing error inconsistent with every other child-count table and with the officially cited $440,000/year combined-income cap -- this calculator uses $290,000 for that final bracket (matching the 1/2/3/5-children pattern exactly) rather than reproducing what appears to be a typo verbatim. Idaho's guidelines do not define a schedule for 6 or more children ('the guidelines income and the children's schedules...are not limitations on child support for more than five children') -- this calculator reuses the 5-children rates for 6+, disclosed as an extrapolation beyond the guideline's own defined range.

deviation_note: This calculator models Idaho's standard Primary Parenting Time (one parent has 25% or less of the overnights, no adjustment) and Shared Physical Custody (both parents have more than 25%, Rule 120(4)(B)'s 1.5x cross-formula) cases. It does not model: Extended Visits (a discretionary further reduction, often 50%, when a 25%-or-less parent has the child for 14+ consecutive days); Split Physical Custody (each parent primary for at least one different child, a separate per-child-multiplier offset calculation, Rule 120(6)); the schedule for 6 or more children (undefined by the guidelines themselves, extrapolated here from the 5-children rates); or discretionary deviations. The $800/month self-support review and $50/child/month minimum are both explicitly described as rebuttable presumptions, not fixed rules.
